_comment: PROPOSAL ONLY — 8 procedural 'build-it-right' scenarios for Domains 1/3/4. Not wired into the app. Validate command coverage against simulators before merging; see audit notes for flagged gaps. All scenarios stay within the 8-node (dgx-00..dgx-07) / 64-GPU topology and use {{PLACEHOLDER}} tokens for architecture-specific values.

scenarios.title: First Light: Bringing a DGX Node Online

narrative.hook: A brand-new {{SYSTEM_NAME}} node just landed in the rack. Nothing is configured yet — your job is to bring it from cold metal to GPU-ready.
narrative.setting: You have out-of-band access to dgx-00 through its BMC. Walk the standard bring-up checklist in order: verify the BMC, confirm a clean POST, then validate that the OS enumerates all 8 {{GPU_MODEL}} GPUs on the PCIe bus.
narrative.resolution: You confirmed the BMC is healthy, the System Event Log is clean, and all 8 GPUs enumerate with {{GPU_MEMORY_GB}}GB each. The node is ready for driver validation.

steps.situation: Every DGX bring-up starts at the BMC — the out-of-band controller that runs before the host OS. It exposes sensors, the System Event Log (SEL), and power control over IPMI.
steps.task: Internalize the bring-up dependency order before touching the hardware.
hints: The BMC is reachable even when the host OS is down.
Each layer depends on the one beneath it.
steps.conceptContent:
Bring-up sequence:
1. BMC / IPMI online (out-of-band)
2. POST + BIOS/firmware checks
3. OS boot + PCIe enumeration
4. GPU driver + CUDA validation
5. NVLink / Fabric Manager
6. Network / InfiniBand fabric
If GPUs don't appear in the OS, suspect POST/PCIe before blaming the driver.
tips: A clean SEL at bring-up gives you a known-good baseline to compare against later.

steps.situation: First, confirm the BMC's view of the chassis is healthy and the event log is clean before the OS even boots.
steps.task: Read the BMC System Event Log to establish a clean baseline.
hints: The SEL records hardware events the OS never sees.
An empty or 'No entries' SEL is what you want on a fresh node.

steps.situation: Power and thermal sensors must read sane values before you trust anything downstream.
steps.task: List the BMC sensor readings and confirm inlet temp and PSU voltages are nominal.
hints: 'ipmitool sensor list' dumps every analog sensor.
Look for Inlet Temp, PSU rails, and fan RPMs in range.

quiz.question: On a freshly-racked node with the host OS not yet booted, which interface can still report chassis temperature and power state?
quiz.explanation: The BMC runs independently of the host OS, so IPMI sensor/power queries work even before (or without) an OS. nvidia-smi, dcgmi and lspci all require a booted OS with the driver/PCIe stack up.

steps.situation: The OS has booted. Before checking the NVIDIA driver, confirm the hardware itself enumerated on the PCIe bus — driver problems and POST problems look identical from nvidia-smi alone.
steps.task: Confirm all 8 GPUs appear as PCIe devices.
hints: lspci lists raw PCIe devices independent of the driver.
You should count 8 GPU entries plus NVSwitch/bridge devices.

steps.situation: PCIe enumeration looks good. Now verify the kernel logged a clean GPU initialization with no fall-off-the-bus or RmInit errors.
steps.task: Scan the kernel ring buffer for NVIDIA driver initialization messages.
hints: 'dmesg | grep -i nvrm' filters NVIDIA kernel messages.
No 'Xid' or 'RmInitAdapter failed' lines is the goal.

steps.situation: Final gate: the user-space tool should now agree with the hardware — 8 healthy GPUs, correct model, correct memory.
steps.task: Run nvidia-smi and confirm 8 {{GPU_MODEL}} GPUs each report {{GPU_MEMORY_GB}}GB.
hints: The header shows the driver/CUDA version; the table lists each GPU.
All 8 should be present with matching memory totals.

quiz.question: lspci shows 8 GPUs but nvidia-smi reports only 7. What is the most likely layer at fault?
options: The driver or a single GPU's RmInit (one GPU failed to initialize)
quiz.explanation: If lspci sees all 8 but nvidia-smi sees 7, the hardware enumerated correctly — so the gap is at the driver/init layer for that one GPU (often an RmInitAdapter failure or an Xid at load). Check dmesg for the specific GPU.

narrative.hook: The node powers on cleanly, but 'powers on' is not 'production-ready.' You need to prove the firmware and software stack are the versions this cluster is standardized on.
narrative.setting: On dgx-00, validate BIOS/baseboard info, the loaded NVIDIA kernel module, the driver/CUDA versions, and that the GPU VBIOS matches the fleet standard.
narrative.resolution: BIOS, driver, CUDA toolkit, and kernel module all match the fleet baseline. The node is approved for fabric and scheduler onboarding.

steps.situation: Firmware validation starts with the baseboard. The DMI tables expose BIOS vendor/version and system serials.
steps.task: Read the BIOS and system information from the DMI tables.
hints: 'dmidecode -t bios' isolates the BIOS section.
Record the BIOS version to compare against the fleet standard.

steps.situation: The NVIDIA kernel module must be loaded before the driver can talk to the GPUs.
steps.task: Confirm the nvidia kernel modules are loaded.
hints: 'lsmod | grep nvidia' shows nvidia, nvidia_uvm, nvidia_drm.
If nothing returns, the driver isn't loaded — stop and investigate.

steps.situation: Now pin down the exact driver version the kernel is running.
steps.task: Read the running driver version from the proc interface.
hints: This proc file is the authoritative running-driver version.
It should match the {{SYSTEM_NAME}} fleet baseline.

quiz.question: Why prefer /proc/driver/nvidia/version over the version printed in the nvidia-smi header?
options: The proc file reflects the kernel module actually loaded right now, which is the source of truth
The proc file shows the CUDA toolkit version
quiz.explanation: Both usually agree, but /proc/driver/nvidia/version is reported directly by the loaded kernel module. After a driver upgrade without reboot, the on-disk userspace and the running module can diverge — the proc file tells you what's truly loaded.

steps.situation: GPU compute work needs a matching CUDA toolkit. Validate the compiler/runtime version.
steps.task: Check the installed CUDA toolkit version.
hints: 'nvcc --version' prints the toolkit release.
The driver must support a CUDA version >= the toolkit's.

steps.situation: Cross-check the driver/CUDA pairing and per-GPU detail from the GPU's own perspective.
steps.task: Use nvidia-smi -q to inspect driver, CUDA, and VBIOS per GPU.
hints: 'nvidia-smi -q' dumps verbose per-GPU detail.
Confirm VBIOS and product name are consistent across all 8 GPUs.

steps.situation: Establish a clean ECC baseline now, while the node is idle, so future error counts are meaningful.
steps.task: Record the current ECC error counts (should be zero on a healthy fresh node).
hints: Look at Aggregate and Volatile, single-bit and double-bit.
All zeros is the baseline you want before handing the node to the scheduler.

narrative.hook: Compute is up, but an isolated node is useless for distributed training. Time to bring the high-speed fabric online.
narrative.setting: dgx-00 has 8 {{NET_PROTOCOL}} HCAs. Verify the Mellanox adapters' firmware/config, bring the links up, and confirm every port reaches {{NET_RATE_GBS}} Gb/s Active before fabric-wide diagnostics.
narrative.resolution: All HCAs report the correct firmware, every port is LinkUp/Active at {{NET_RATE_GBS}} Gb/s, and ibdiagnet finds no fabric errors. The node is fabric-ready.

steps.situation: Mellanox adapters are managed through the MST (Mellanox Software Tools) device layer. Enumerate them first.
steps.task: Start MST and list the discovered Mellanox devices.
hints: 'mst start' loads the MST kernel module.
'mst status' lists the /dev/mst/* device paths you'll use with mlxconfig.

steps.situation: Confirm the adapter firmware and key configuration (link type set to InfiniBand, not Ethernet) before bringing links up.
steps.task: Query a Mellanox adapter's configuration.
hints: 'mlxconfig -d <device> q' queries current config.
LINK_TYPE_P1 should be IB for an InfiniBand fabric.

steps.situation: Now verify each HCA port at the link layer.
steps.task: Check InfiniBand adapter status and confirm ports are Active.
hints: 'ibstat' shows per-HCA, per-port State and Rate.
State should be Active and Physical state LinkUp.

quiz.question: ibstat shows a port as 'Initializing' rather than 'Active.' What does that usually indicate?
options: The physical link is up but the Subnet Manager hasn't assigned the port a LID yet
quiz.explanation: 'Initializing' means the physical link trained (LinkUp) but the Subnet Manager hasn't yet brought the port to Active by programming its LID/routing. A missing or unreachable SM is the classic cause of ports stuck in Initializing.

steps.situation: Confirm the link width and speed are what the {{NET_PROTOCOL}} standard expects — a port can be Active but degraded to a narrower width.
steps.task: Inspect link info to confirm full width and {{NET_RATE_GBS}} Gb/s rate.
hints: 'iblinkinfo' shows width (e.g. 4X) and speed per link.
A 1X width at full speed is still a degraded link — watch for it.

steps.situation: With local ports healthy, run a fabric-wide diagnostic sweep to catch errors anywhere in the topology.
steps.task: Run ibdiagnet and confirm a clean fabric report.
hints: 'ibdiagnet' walks the whole fabric and summarizes errors.
Zero symbol errors and a consistent SM is the pass condition.

steps.situation: Finally, establish a port-error baseline so future stress tests have something to compare against.
steps.task: Read performance counters to record a zero-error baseline.
hints: 'perfquery' dumps port error/data counters.
Note SymbolErrors and LinkErrorRecovery are 0 before load.

narrative.hook: Eight GPUs in one node only reach full bandwidth if the NVSwitch fabric is initialized. That's the Fabric Manager's job.
narrative.setting: On dgx-00, start and verify the NVIDIA Fabric Manager service, confirm it programmed the NVSwitches, and validate the NVLink topology shows full GPU-to-GPU connectivity.
narrative.resolution: Fabric Manager is active, all NVSwitches are configured, and the NVLink topology matrix shows every GPU connected to every other GPU at {{NVLINK_BW_GBS}} GB/s. Intra-node collectives can now hit full NVLink bandwidth.

steps.situation: On NVSwitch-based DGX systems, GPUs can't reach each other over NVLink until the Fabric Manager programs the switch routing. Without it, NVLink falls back to degraded or disabled paths.
steps.task: Understand why Fabric Manager is mandatory before NVLink collectives.
hints: NVSwitch is a hardware crossbar; FM is the software that configures it.
No FM → no full all-to-all NVLink bandwidth.
steps.conceptContent:
Fabric Manager (nvidia-fabricmanager):
- Runs as a systemd service on the head GPU node.
- Discovers NVSwitches and GPUs, then programs switch routing tables.
- Must be Active before NCCL/CUDA can use full NVLink all-to-all paths.
If FM is stopped, nvidia-smi nvlink may show links down and collective bandwidth collapses to PCIe levels.
tips: FM failing to start is often a GPU/NVSwitch mismatch or a driver/FM version skew.

steps.situation: Check whether the Fabric Manager service is running.
steps.task: Query the nvidia-fabricmanager service status.
hints: 'systemctl status nvidia-fabricmanager' shows active/inactive.
You want 'active (running)'.

steps.situation: If it isn't running, start it; if it is, you'll confirm idempotently.
steps.task: Start (or confirm) the Fabric Manager service.
hints: Start the service, then re-check status.
FM must initialize before NVLink queries are meaningful.

steps.situation: Confirm the Fabric Manager actually configured the NVSwitches.
steps.task: Query Fabric Manager / NVSwitch state.
hints: nv-fabricmanager reports NVSwitch configuration state.
All switches should report configured/healthy.

steps.situation: Verify the NVLink topology matrix — every GPU should reach every other GPU over NVLink (NV# links), not PCIe (PIX/SYS).
steps.task: Print the GPU topology matrix.
hints: 'nvidia-smi topo -m' prints the GPU-to-GPU link matrix.
Off-diagonal cells should show NV# (NVLink), not SYS/PHB.

quiz.question: In 'nvidia-smi topo -m', a GPU-to-GPU cell reads 'SYS' instead of 'NV8'. What does that tell you on an NVSwitch system with Fabric Manager supposedly running?
options: Those GPUs are communicating over the system/PCIe path instead of NVLink — likely an NVLink or Fabric Manager problem
The GPUs are in different nodes
quiz.explanation: SYS means traffic traverses the CPU/PCIe path between sockets — far slower than NVLink. On an NVSwitch box that should show NV#, a SYS cell signals downed NVLinks or a Fabric Manager that didn't program the switches.

steps.situation: Confirm individual NVLink status and that no links report errors.
steps.task: Check NVLink link status.
hints: 'nvidia-smi nvlink -s' lists per-link state and speed.
All {{NVLINK_LINKS_PER_GPU}} links per GPU should be active.

scenarios.title: Standing Up the Scheduler: Slurm Partitions & Health

narrative.hook: The cluster has compute and fabric — now it needs a scheduler so users can actually request GPUs.
narrative.setting: Bring the 8-node cluster (dgx-00..dgx-07, 64 GPUs) under Slurm: confirm node registration, inspect the GPU partition, run a node health check, and submit a trivial GPU job to prove the GRES wiring works.
narrative.resolution: All 8 nodes register idle, the gpu partition exposes 64 GPUs via GRES, a health check passes, and a test job lands on a node and returns. The scheduler is production-ready.

steps.situation: Start by confirming Slurm sees every node and what state they're in.
steps.task: List the cluster's nodes and partitions.
hints: 'sinfo' summarizes partitions and node states.
All 8 nodes should be 'idle' on a fresh bring-up.

steps.situation: Inspect a node's registered resources to confirm Slurm knows it has 8 GPUs (GRES).
steps.task: Show the detailed configuration for dgx-00.
hints: 'scontrol show node dgx-00' prints CfgTRES/Gres.
Look for gpu:8 in the GRES line.

quiz.question: A node is physically healthy with 8 GPUs, but 'scontrol show node' lists Gres=gpu:0. What is the consequence?
options: Jobs requesting --gres=gpu will never schedule on that node
quiz.explanation: Slurm only allocates resources it knows about. If GRES is misconfigured to gpu:0, the scheduler believes the node has no GPUs, so any --gres=gpu request will skip it — a classic bring-up misconfiguration.

steps.situation: Before opening the cluster to users, run a health check sweep across the nodes.
steps.task: Run a GPU diagnostic to confirm node health.
hints: 'dcgmi diag -r 1' is the fast pass/fail health check.
All checks should pass on a clean node.

steps.situation: Now prove the GRES plumbing end-to-end by submitting a trivial GPU job.
steps.task: Submit a batch job that requests one GPU.
hints: A --gres=gpu:1 request exercises the scheduler's GPU binding.
sbatch returns a job ID on success.

steps.situation: Watch the job move through the queue.
steps.task: Check the job queue.
hints: 'squeue' shows pending/running jobs.
Your job should be R (running) or quickly complete.

steps.situation: Finally, confirm the job's accounting record shows it consumed a GPU.
steps.task: Inspect completed-job accounting.
hints: 'sacct' reads the accounting database.
AllocTRES should include gres/gpu=1 for the job.

scenarios.title: Container Runtime: Docker, Enroot & Pyxis with NGC

narrative.hook: Researchers want to run NGC containers under Slurm. You need the GPU-aware container stack wired up.
narrative.setting: On the cluster, validate Docker GPU access, pull an NGC image, convert it for unprivileged use with Enroot, and launch it through Slurm via Pyxis.
narrative.resolution: Docker sees the GPUs, an NGC image is cached, Enroot produced a squashfs, and a Pyxis srun launched the container with GPU access. The container workflow is ready for users.

steps.situation: First confirm the Docker runtime can expose GPUs to containers.
steps.task: Run a container with GPU access and list the GPUs from inside it.
hints: '--gpus all' hands every GPU to the container.
nvidia-smi -L inside the container should list all 8 GPUs.

steps.situation: Production images come from NGC (NVIDIA GPU Cloud). Confirm the NGC CLI is configured and can see the registry.
steps.task: List available NGC container images.
hints: The NGC CLI talks to nvcr.io.
You're confirming auth/connectivity before pulling.

quiz.question: Why is Enroot preferred over Docker for launching containers on a shared Slurm/HPC login?
options: Enroot is faster at compiling code
Enroot runs containers unprivileged (no root daemon), which fits multi-tenant HPC security and Slurm integration via Pyxis
quiz.explanation: Docker requires a privileged daemon, which is undesirable on shared HPC login/compute nodes. Enroot runs rootless and integrates with Slurm through the Pyxis SPANK plugin, so users launch containers as themselves under the scheduler.

steps.situation: Convert an NGC image into an Enroot squashfs for unprivileged execution.
steps.task: Import an NGC image with Enroot.
hints: 'enroot import docker://...' pulls and converts to a .sqsh.
The docker://nvcr.io# prefix targets the NGC registry.

steps.situation: Create a writable container root from the imported image.
steps.task: Create an Enroot container.
hints: 'enroot create' unpacks the squashfs into a container.
Name it so you can start it by name.

steps.situation: Confirm the container exists in the local list.
steps.task: List Enroot containers.
hints: 'enroot list' shows created containers.
Your named container should appear.

steps.situation: Now the payoff: launch the container through Slurm with Pyxis so it runs on a compute node with GPUs.
steps.task: Use srun with the Pyxis --container-image flag to run a GPU container.
hints: Pyxis adds --container-image to srun.
Combine with --gres=gpu:1 to get a GPU inside the container.

narrative.hook: The cluster is built. Before it certifies for training, you must prove the GPUs can actually talk to each other at full bandwidth.
narrative.setting: Run the NCCL all-reduce bandwidth test across the 8-node / 64-GPU cluster, interpret the bus-bandwidth curve, and confirm it approaches the expected {{NVLINK_BW_GBS}} GB/s intra-node and {{NET_RATE_GBS}} Gb/s inter-node ceilings.
narrative.resolution: The all-reduce bus bandwidth plateaus near the hardware ceiling at large message sizes, with no stragglers. The interconnect is validated for distributed training.

steps.situation: NCCL all-reduce is the canonical collective in data-parallel training. Its bus bandwidth at large messages is the truest single measure of interconnect health.
steps.task: Understand algorithm vs. bus bandwidth before running the test.
hints: Small messages are latency-bound; large messages are bandwidth-bound.
Bus bandwidth normalizes for the all-reduce data-movement factor.
steps.conceptContent:
NCCL test output columns:
- size (bytes), count, type
- time (us), algbw (GB/s), busbw (GB/s)
busbw accounts for the 2(N-1)/N data movement of ring all-reduce, so it's comparable to the hardware link bandwidth. Read the busbw at the largest message size and compare it to your NVLink/IB ceiling. A low plateau or a single slow rank points at a degraded link.
tips: Always sweep a wide size range (-b small -e large); a single size hides the latency-vs-bandwidth transition.

steps.situation: Start with a single-node, 8-GPU run to validate intra-node NVLink before adding the network.
steps.task: Run all-reduce across 8 GPUs on one node, sweeping message sizes.
hints: -b/-e set the begin/end message sizes; -g sets GPU count.
Intra-node busbw should approach the NVLink ceiling.

steps.situation: Now scale across nodes. Multi-node collectives go over InfiniBand, so this validates the fabric end-to-end.
steps.task: Launch a multi-node all-reduce via mpirun across the cluster.
hints: -np 64 spans all 64 ranks; -g 1 = one GPU per rank.
Inter-node busbw is bounded by {{NET_RATE_GBS}} Gb/s per HCA.

quiz.question: Single-node busbw is excellent, but multi-node busbw is ~1/10th of it. Where do you look first?
options: The InfiniBand fabric — link rate/width, SM, and whether NCCL is actually using IB vs falling back to TCP
quiz.explanation: A large single-node vs multi-node gap isolates the problem to the inter-node path: degraded IB links, a missing/over-subscribed Subnet Manager, or NCCL silently falling back to sockets (NCCL_IB_DISABLE / no GPUDirect). Intra-node NVLink is clearly fine.

steps.situation: If bandwidth looks low, confirm NCCL chose the InfiniBand transport and the links are clean.
steps.task: Re-check IB port counters for errors accumulated during the run.
hints: Compare error counters to the bring-up baseline.
Rising SymbolErrors during the test indicate a marginal link/cable.

steps.situation: Cross-check that no GPU was thermally throttling during the collective, which would cap bandwidth.
steps.task: Inspect GPU clocks/temps for throttle during load.
hints: A throttled GPU lowers SM clocks and drags the slowest rank.
Temps should be well under the thermal limit.

scenarios.title: Validation: DCGM Diagnostic Levels & ECC Baseline

narrative.hook: Acceptance testing time. The cluster must pass a structured GPU diagnostic before it certifies for production.
narrative.setting: Use DCGM's three diagnostic levels on the cluster: a fast deployment check, a medium stress pass, and the full hardware/stress suite — then establish the ECC error baseline and a health-watch policy.
narrative.resolution: All three DCGM levels pass, ECC counts are zero across the fleet, and a health policy is registered to flag future regressions. The cluster is certified.

steps.situation: DCGM's `diag -r` levels trade runtime for coverage. Knowing what each level actually exercises tells you which to run when.
steps.task: Understand the three diagnostic run levels.
hints: Level 1 ≈ seconds; Level 3 ≈ many minutes of stress.
Use 1 for quick gating, 3 for acceptance.
steps.conceptContent:
DCGM diagnostic levels:
- -r 1 (Quick/Deployment): software/config sanity — driver, NVML, persistence, page-retirement. Seconds.
- -r 2 (Medium): adds integration + light stress (PCIe, memory bandwidth). Minutes.
- -r 3 (Long): full stress — SM stress, targeted power/thermal, memory stress, NVLink. Tens of minutes; surfaces marginal hardware.
Run 1 at every bring-up; run 3 for acceptance and after hardware changes.
tips: A node that passes -r 1 but fails -r 3 usually has marginal hardware that only shows under sustained load.

steps.situation: Start with the fast deployment check.
steps.task: Run the DCGM Level 1 diagnostic.
hints: This validates the software/config layer quickly.
Every row should report Pass.

steps.situation: Escalate to the medium suite to exercise PCIe and memory bandwidth.
steps.task: Run the DCGM Level 2 diagnostic.
hints: Level 2 adds integration + light stress.
Watch for PCIe or memory-bandwidth warnings.

steps.situation: Run the full stress suite for acceptance certification.
steps.task: Run the DCGM Level 3 diagnostic.
hints: Level 3 is the long, full-stress pass.
This is the one that catches marginal silicon and NVLink issues.

quiz.question: DCGM -r 3 reports a 'Memory Bandwidth' subtest failure on one GPU while -r 1 passed. What's the right interpretation?
options: The driver is the wrong version
A marginal hardware fault on that GPU that only manifests under sustained stress — quarantine/RMA candidate
The whole cluster is fine; ignore it
quiz.explanation: Level 1 only checks software/config, which passed — so the stack is fine. A Level 3 stress subtest failure isolates a load-dependent hardware fault on that specific GPU. Drain the node and treat the GPU as a quarantine/RMA candidate.

steps.situation: Confirm the ECC baseline is clean after stress — stress testing can surface latent memory faults.
steps.task: Read aggregate ECC counts across the GPUs.
hints: Post-stress ECC should still be zero double-bit.
Single-bit errors are corrected but worth trending.

steps.situation: Finally, register a health policy so the cluster auto-flags future ECC/thermal/XID regressions.
steps.task: Set a DCGM health/policy watch.
hints: dcgmi policy registers conditions (ECC, thermal, XID).
A registered watch turns silent regressions into alerts.
